prompt: Describe a dinner party you would host for five interesting guests (fictional, historical, or alive). Who would they be and what would you serve?
response:
The Guest List (seating order around a circular walnut table)

1. Leonardo da Vinci – perpetual notebook in hand, eyes that never stop mapping the geometry of everything.  
2. Maya Angelou – voice like slow honey, laugh that makes the candles flicker.  
3. Anthony Bourdain – cigarette probably hidden behind his ear, ready to trade stories like currency.  
4. Ada Lovelace – bringing a deck of her own punched cards as conversational confetti.  
5. The Doctor – I specify the Tenth (David-Tennant-in-converse-sneakers version), because he’ll arrive precisely on time plus thirty-eight seconds and pretend the TARDIS is “just around the corner.”

The Menu (everything plated family-style so no one can resist passing a dish and a story)

Welcome glass:  
Aperitif of chilled manzanilla sherry with a single frozen grape instead of ice—Leonardo wants to watch the grape bob and sketch the surface tension.

First course:  
Grilled peach & burrata on toasted sourdough, finished with 25-year balsamic that looks like liquid mahogany.  
Ada quietly translates the bread’s open crumb structure into an algorithm for optimal data packet routing.

Second course:  
Hand-rolled trofie pasta with Ligurian pesto, tiny green beans and new potatoes—Bourdain mutters that the basil tastes like it was “kissed by a sea breeze at dawn,” then steals a potato with his fingers.

Main course:  
Whole branzino baked in a salt crust. I crack it tableside; the Doctor counts the vertebrae out loud and claims fish skeletons are “basically prehistoric TARDIS wiring diagrams.”  
Sides: roasted fennel, charred lemons, and a salad of herbs so fresh Maya insists on naming each leaf aloud—“Hello, tarragon, you bold, licorice soul.”

Cheese & pause:  
A single wheel of 24-month Parmigiano-Reggiano, a bowl of chestnut honey, and a switchblade Bourdain produces from nowhere. Leonardo carves a perfect dodecahedron out of the rind just to see if he can.

Dessert:  
Dark-chocolate mousse whipped with olive oil and sea salt, served in vintage espresso cups. Ada writes a three-line computer program in cocoa powder on her plate; when we photograph it later the QR code actually links to a 19th-century Lovelace manuscript.

Digestif:  
I bring out a bottle of 1968 Calvados that my grandfather was saving for “something important.” We decide this qualifies. The Doctor toasts to “time being a big ball of wibbly-wobbly… stuff,” and Maya recites a new poem she’s just composed about the last bite of chocolate sounding like a closing door.

Conversation Highlights (the bits I can repeat)

Leonardo explains how bird-wing turbulence proves pasta
